ideas.Air as the primary substance:
Anaximenes of Miletus, the third of the great Milesian philosophers (after Thales and Anaximander), proposed that **air** (*aēr*) is the fundamental principle (*archē*) — the single underlying substance from which all things come to be and of which all things are ultimately composed. In choosing air, Anaximenes returned to the approach of Thales, who had named a *definite, observable* substance (water) as the source of everything, rather than the abstract, indefinite **apeiron** of his teacher Anaximander.

Air was a shrewd choice, arguably combining the virtues of both his predecessors. Like Thales's water, it is a familiar, concrete, natural element that we can observe. But air also shares something of the character of Anaximander's boundless: it is invisible, all-pervading, seemingly limitless in extent, and formless in itself — closer to the "indefinite" while still being a *specific* kind of stuff. Air thus offered a first principle that was both determinate and yet vast, subtle, and encompassing.

Anaximenes seems to have found support for the primacy of air in the phenomenon of **life and breath**. Just as our own breath (*pneuma*) and the air is what holds our soul together and gives us life — "as our soul, being air, holds us together and controls us, so do breath and air surround the whole cosmos" — so air, on a cosmic scale, is the living, animating principle that encompasses and governs the whole world. Air is the source of life and the very stuff of the soul. By identifying a single, all-pervading, life-giving material principle underlying the diversity of nature, Anaximenes continued and refined the Milesian search for the one substance behind the many — and, crucially, added a compelling account of *how* the one becomes the many.
ideas.Condensation and rarefaction as the engi:
Anaximenes's most important and original contribution — and arguably his decisive advance over both Thales and Anaximander — was his account of **how** a single primary substance can transform into the vast diversity of things in the world. His answer was the elegant, mechanical process of **condensation** (thickening, *pyknōsis*) and **rarefaction** (thinning, *manōsis*). This gave the Milesian program something it had crucially lacked: a clear, quantitative explanation of *change* and of the generation of different substances from one.

The problem for any monist who proposes a single *archē* is to explain how that one substance produces the many different kinds of things we experience — earth, water, fire, mist, stone. Thales and Anaximander had not clearly specified the *mechanism* of transformation. Anaximenes solved this by proposing that the differences between substances are differences of **density**, produced by the compression or expansion of the underlying air. When air is **rarefied** (thinned out), it becomes hotter and turns into **fire**; when it is **condensed** (thickened), it becomes progressively colder and denser, turning first into **wind**, then **cloud**, then **water**, then **earth**, and finally **stone**. All substances thus lie on a single continuum of density, differing only in how much air has been packed together or spread apart.

The importance of this idea is hard to overstate. It reduces *qualitative* differences (the many kinds of stuff) to a single *quantitative* variable (density), explained by a natural, mechanical process. It thereby gives a genuinely *unified* physical theory in which one substance and one kind of process account for all the phenomena of nature — a powerful step toward the scientific ideal of explaining diverse phenomena by underlying mechanical causes.
ideas.A unified material principle:
Anaximenes brought the Milesian search for a **unified material principle** to a kind of culmination, offering the most complete and satisfying version of the project begun by Thales and continued by Anaximander: the reduction of the whole diversity of nature to a *single* underlying substance governed by a *single* process of change. In this, he crystallized the founding idea of natural philosophy — that beneath the endless variety of the world there lies one fundamental, unifying reality.

The three Milesians shared a common ambition — to identify the *archē*, the one basic stuff and origin of all things — but each refined the enterprise. Thales proposed **water**, a definite substance, but left the mechanism of transformation obscure. Anaximander proposed the abstract, indefinite **apeiron**, gaining generality but at the cost of positing an unobservable, indeterminate source. Anaximenes achieved a powerful synthesis: he chose **air**, a substance that is *definite* and observable (like Thales's water) yet also subtle, invisible, and all-encompassing (approaching the character of the boundless), *and* he supplied the missing mechanism — **condensation and rarefaction** — that explains precisely how the one substance becomes the many.

The result is a remarkably complete and coherent **material monism**: a single substance (air), a single explanatory process (change of density), and a naturalistic account of the entire cosmos, from the elements to the heavenly bodies to the soul. Everything is, at bottom, air in different states of compression. This vision — that all the qualitative variety of nature can be understood as modifications of one underlying material principle through natural processes — is a foundational moment in the history of science. It expresses, in its clearest early form, the enduring conviction that the world is fundamentally *one* and rationally intelligible.

works.On Nature (fragments):
*On Nature* (*Peri Physeōs*) is the title traditionally given to the treatise of Anaximenes of Miletus, the third of the great Milesian natural philosophers — a work that, like those of his predecessors Thales and Anaximander, has been **lost**, surviving only through scattered **fragments** and the reports (*testimonia*) of later ancient authors. Ancient sources noted that Anaximenes wrote in a simple, unadorned prose style, plainer than Anaximander's, suited to his sober, explanatory purpose.

Though the book itself is gone, the reports preserve the essentials of its doctrine, which represents the mature culmination of the Milesian tradition. From these sources we learn of Anaximenes's central teaching: that **air** (*aēr*) is the fundamental principle (*archē*) of all things — the single, all-pervading, life-giving substance from which everything arises. And, crucially, we learn of his signal innovation: the explanation of how air transforms into all other substances through the twin mechanical processes of **condensation** (thickening) and **rarefaction** (thinning), so that rarefied air becomes fire, and progressively condensed air becomes wind, cloud, water, earth, and stone.

The reports also indicate that the treatise, in the comprehensive manner of the Milesian *On Nature* works, extended to cosmology and astronomy (with Anaximenes offering accounts of the earth, the heavenly bodies as fiery, and their motions), to meteorological phenomena (thunder, lightning, rainbows, hail, explained naturalistically), and to the nature of the soul as air. One important surviving fragment draws the famous analogy between the soul and the cosmos: "as our soul, being air, holds us together and controls us, so do breath and air surround the whole cosmos." Through such fragments and testimonia, the lost book still transmits the influential vision that completed the founding project of Greek natural philosophy.
